quotes.quote: To ensure the proposals are both grounded and new, this process is tightly integrated with a literature review module that queries external academic databases to identify knowledge gaps and avoid rediscovering existing work. The system uses structured prompts to guide idea generation and reflection rounds to refine proposals based on the literature search results (see Supplementary Information section A.2.6 for prompts).
quotes.rationale: Identifies an internal literature-review evaluator used to judge groundedness and novelty and route results into proposal refinement.

quotes.quote: At the end of each stage, an LLM-based evaluator assesses all leaf nodes and selects the most promising one to serve as the root for the next stage of exploration, thus effectively pruning less promising research avenues.
quotes.rationale: Describes a distinct LLM evaluator that ranks leaf nodes and controls the next-stage root, causing pruning.

quotes.quote: During experimentation, the plots generated are fed to a VLM, which is prompted to act as a scientist and critique them. For example, it might flag nonsensical axes or issues in the quality of generated examples or suggest clearer ways to present the data. This feedback is used to generate new experimental nodes in the tree search aimed at addressing the identified issues.
quotes.rationale: Identifies a VLM critique channel whose diagnostics are routed into new experimental nodes.

quotes.quote: When choosing between non-buggy nodes, the system uses a best-first search strategy guided by GPT-4o, which evaluates candidates based on factors like performance metrics, training dynamics and the quality of the plots generated. The selected nodes are expanded by creating a new child node.
quotes.rationale: Describes GPT-4o as the evaluator guiding best-first search among non-buggy nodes using metrics, training dynamics, and plots.

quotes.quote: The agent processes the PDF of a manuscript to produce a structured review, including numerical scores for soundness, presentation and contribution, along with a list of strengths and weaknesses and a preliminary accept or reject decision (Supplementary Information section A.3).
quotes.rationale: Defines the automated reviewer output as manuscript-level scores, strengths and weaknesses, and a preliminary accept or reject decision.

quotes.quote: We benchmarked The Automated Reviewer against human decisions using ICLR data from the publicly available OpenReview dataset33. The Automated Reviewer achieved a comparable balanced accuracy with humans (69% versus 66%; see Supplementary Information section A.3.2 for details) and a higher F1 score compared with inter-human group agreement (0.62 versus 0.49) in the NeurIPS 2021 consistency experiment34, for which roughly 10% of submissions were randomly selected and sent to two independent review committees, thus providing a real-world benchmark of inter-reviewer consistency (Table 1).
quotes.rationale: Reports discrimination and agreement evidence for the automated reviewer against human decisions and inter-reviewer consistency benchmarks.

quotes.quote: The overall process was then run to generate ideas, experiments and papers. We manually filtered the most promising outputs at each stage (Supplementary Information section A.4). Had this filtering not occurred, the papers under analysis would still have been produced in their final form, just along with other papers and, thus, at a greater total cost. This process resulted in three complete manuscripts being selected for submission.
quotes.rationale: Reports human filtering of outputs at each stage and selection of three manuscripts for submission.

quotes.quote: The entire scientific workflow for each paper, from ideation and coding to manuscript writing, was performed without any human modification.
quotes.rationale: Constrains the human role by stating no human modification of the scientific workflow outputs.

quotes.quote: These three submissions were included among the 43 papers reviewed for the workshop. Reviewers were informed that some of the submissions were AI-generated but not which ones, ensuring a blind process.
quotes.rationale: Describes an external blind workshop review process for the selected submissions.

quotes.quote: Aggregation nodes are special nodes created to consolidate and visualize the combined results of replication nodes. Unlike other node types, aggregation nodes do not conduct new experiments but simply generate a Python script to aggregate and summarize previous results.
quotes.rationale: Identifies an aggregation mechanism that consolidates and visualizes replication-node results without conducting new experiments.

diagnostics.consequence: Search decisions can prune or expand research paths based on unvalidated internal LLM and VLM judgments, limiting confidence that selected paths are better than discarded alternatives.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: At the end of each stage, an LLM-based evaluator assesses all leaf nodes and selects the most promising one to serve as the root for the next stage of exploration, thus effectively pruning less promising research avenues.

During experimentation, the plots generated are fed to a VLM, which is prompted to act as a scientist and critique them. For example, it might flag nonsensical axes or issues in the quality of generated examples or suggest clearer ways to present the data. This feedback is used to generate new experimental nodes in the tree search aimed at addressing the identified issues.

When choosing between non-buggy nodes, the system uses a best-first search strategy guided by GPT-4o, which evaluates candidates based on factors like performance metrics, training dynamics and the quality of the plots generated. The selected nodes are expanded by creating a new child node.

diagnostics.statement: The packet reports internal LLM and VLM channels controlling pruning, expansion, and best-first search, but reports no reliability study for those exact search-control channels.

diagnostics.consequence: The reproducibility and interpretation of the selected submissions are affected because human selection shaped which outputs were submitted while the selection criteria and reliability are not evidenced in the packet.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: The overall process was then run to generate ideas, experiments and papers. We manually filtered the most promising outputs at each stage (Supplementary Information section A.4). Had this filtering not occurred, the papers under analysis would still have been produced in their final form, just along with other papers and, thus, at a greater total cost. This process resulted in three complete manuscripts being selected for submission.
The entire scientific workflow for each paper, from ideation and coding to manuscript writing, was performed without any human modification.

diagnostics.statement: Human filtering selected promising outputs and submissions, while the packet also states the scientific workflow had no human modification; the boundary between selection authority and workflow autonomy is therefore material and only partly specified.

diagnostics.consequence: Claims supported by the reported workflow should be interpreted within computational-experiment fidelity unless future non-computational execution is separately demonstrated.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: At present, The AI Scientist conducts computational experiments only. In future work, this same playbook could be applied to other scientific domains where one can automatically conduct experiments (or have humans conduct them) and collect data from them (for example, automated chemistry laboratories, on which swift progress is being made46).

diagnostics.statement: The packet explicitly limits the present system to computational experiments and describes other scientific domains as future work.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: At the end of each stage, an LLM-based evaluator assesses all leaf nodes and selects the most promising one to serve as the root for the next stage of exploration, thus effectively pruning less promising research avenues.

During experimentation, the plots generated are fed to a VLM, which is prompted to act as a scientist and critique them. For example, it might flag nonsensical axes or issues in the quality of generated examples or suggest clearer ways to present the data. This feedback is used to generate new experimental nodes in the tree search aimed at addressing the identified issues.

When choosing between non-buggy nodes, the system uses a best-first search strategy guided by GPT-4o, which evaluates candidates based on factors like performance metrics, training dynamics and the quality of the plots generated. The selected nodes are expanded by creating a new child node.

recommendations.proposed_action: Report channel-specific validation for the LLM and VLM search-control decisions, comparing selected, pruned, and expanded nodes on downstream outcomes.

success_check.comparator_or_threshold: Selected or expanded nodes outperform pruned alternatives on predeclared downstream metrics more often than a stated baseline policy.
success_check.evidence_needed: A retained audit log of candidate nodes, evaluator decisions, and downstream outcomes for the stage-end, VLM critique, and GPT-4o best-first channels.
success_check.observable: Per-channel validation table for pruning, expansion, and best-first selection decisions.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: The overall process was then run to generate ideas, experiments and papers. We manually filtered the most promising outputs at each stage (Supplementary Information section A.4). Had this filtering not occurred, the papers under analysis would still have been produced in their final form, just along with other papers and, thus, at a greater total cost. This process resulted in three complete manuscripts being selected for submission.
The entire scientific workflow for each paper, from ideation and coding to manuscript writing, was performed without any human modification.

recommendations.proposed_action: Document the manual filtering protocol and preserve stage-level selection logs separating human selection from human modification.

success_check.comparator_or_threshold: Every manually filtered stage has a recorded candidate set, selection rationale, and confirmation that selected manuscripts were not modified by humans.
success_check.evidence_needed: Stage-level filtering logs and submission selection records aligned with workflow artifact histories.
success_check.observable: Auditable trace from generated outputs to the three selected submissions.

exact_quotes.exact_quote: At present, The AI Scientist conducts computational experiments only. In future work, this same playbook could be applied to other scientific domains where one can automatically conduct experiments (or have humans conduct them) and collect data from them (for example, automated chemistry laboratories, on which swift progress is being made46).

recommendations.proposed_action: Scope reported conclusions to computational experiments unless a non-computational experiment channel is exercised and documented.

success_check.comparator_or_threshold: Non-computational claims are either absent or paired with exercised robotic, physical-assay, or human-conducted experiment evidence.
success_check.evidence_needed: A claims audit plus experiment records showing the fidelity of any non-computational domain demonstration.
success_check.observable: Explicit fidelity label attached to each reported domain-level claim.
